name: Microsoft Seeing AI
short_description: A free smartphone app that narrates the visual world for blind and low-vision users — reading text, identifying people, products, currency, and scenes through on-device and cloud AI.
long_description: Seeing AI turns a smartphone camera into a real-time audio narrator for people who are blind or have low vision. Launched on iOS in July 2017 and Android in late 2023, it was developed at Microsoft Garage by a team led by Saqib Shaikh (a blind software engineer) and originated from a 2014 hackathon project. The app routes the camera feed through specialized channels — Short Text, Documents, Products (barcode), Scenes, People, Currency, Colors, Handwriting — each optimized for a different class of visual query. Some channels run on-device for speed; richer scene descriptions call cloud vision APIs. As a genealogical artifact it shows what the convergence of smartphone cameras, on-device deep learning, and cloud cognitive services can do specifically for accessibility — an interaction paradigm in which the AI observes on behalf of its user rather than augmenting sighted perception.
ai_note: Computer vision and deep learning run on-device for text and barcode tasks; cloud Vision APIs power scene description and face recognition.

images.caption: Seeing AI app interface showing the channel selector for text, documents, products, and scenes.
images.alt_text: A smartphone screen showing the Seeing AI app with a live camera viewfinder and a row of channel buttons at the bottom labeled Short Text, Document, Product, Scene, and Person.

sources.title: Seeing AI
sources.access_notes: Official product site; was formerly at microsoft.com/en-us/ai/seeing-ai (301 redirects here).

sources.title: Seeing AI — Microsoft Garage Wall of Fame
sources.access_notes: Documents the app's Garage origins, hackathon backstory, technology (vision-to-language + image classification), and impact statistics (3M+ tasks by end of 2017).

sources.title: Seeing AI App Launches on Android — Including new and updated features and new languages
sources.access_notes: Announces Android launch (December 2023), 18 supported languages, Document Chat, and richer photo descriptions.

sources.title: Seeing AI
sources.access_notes: Background on initial iOS release (July 12, 2017), feature timeline, and currency/language expansion milestones.

sources.title: Seeing AI — Talking Camera App for the Blind Community (Core77 Design Awards 2018)
sources.access_notes: Design for Social Impact Award entry; documents 150,000 downloads and 5M+ tasks completed as of 2018 submission.

sources.title: Seeing AI — App Store
sources.access_notes: App Store listing; confirms version 5.6.1 (2025), 19 supported languages, and LiDAR World channel added in iOS 14 era.

interactions.title: Read a receipt without asking for help
interactions.description: Hold the phone over a printed receipt and Seeing AI reads each line aloud — items, prices, total — using the Document channel's audio guidance to straighten and capture the page, giving a user who is blind independence at checkout.

interactions.title: Scan a barcode to know what's in the cupboard
interactions.description: Wave the phone over a tin or box and audible beeps speed up as the barcode nears the frame; once locked it announces the product name and description, replacing a $1,300 dedicated scanner.

interactions.title: Identify a face and hear their mood
interactions.description: Point the camera at a person and the app names them if they are a saved contact, or describes a stranger's approximate age, gender, and facial expression, drawing on on-device facial recognition.

interactions.title: Ask a follow-up question about a document
interactions.description: After scanning a document, type or speak a follow-up — 'What is the total?' or 'Summarize this' — and the Document Chat feature answers from the captured content, extending the interaction past a single read-aloud.

interactions.title: Navigate a room with audio augmented reality
interactions.description: On LiDAR-equipped iPhones the World channel generates spatial audio cues — sounds that shift as you turn — so a user can locate a chair, a door, or a person without touching anything.

interactions.title: Teach the app a face at a party
interactions.description: A blind user photographs a friend or colleague, assigns a name, and from then on Seeing AI names that person whenever they appear in frame — turning one-time enrollment into a persistent recognition shortcut.
interactions.source_label: Perkins School for the Blind — Seeing AI review

interactions.title: Decode color-coded charts in a document
interactions.description: Point the phone at a chart, invoke the Colors channel to identify the exact hue of each segment, then switch to Document Chat to ask what each color represents — combining two channels to parse information normally visual.

interactions.title: Read a medicine label before taking the dose
interactions.description: Hold the phone over a prescription bottle; the Short Text channel reads the drug name, dosage, and instructions aloud — grounded in the same real-time OCR the app already runs for receipts and signs.
